popular_quote: To err is human; to forgive, divine.
author: Alexander Pope
snippet_in_source: To err is human; to forgive, divine.

popular_quote: A little learning is a dangerous thing.
author: Alexander Pope
discrepancy_note: Often misquoted as 'A little knowledge is a dangerous thing'; original is 'learning'.
snippet_in_source: A little learning is a dang'rous thing;

popular_quote: Hope springs eternal in the human breast.
author: Alexander Pope
discrepancy_note: Identical to original; popular shorthand 'Hope springs eternal' drops the rest.
snippet_in_source: Hope springs eternal in the human breast:

popular_quote: The Child is father of the Man.
author: William Wordsworth

popular_quote: Things fall apart; the centre cannot hold.
author: W.B. Yeats
snippet_in_source: Things fall apart; the centre cannot hold;

popular_quote: 'Tis better to have loved and lost than never to have loved at all.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

popular_quote: Nature, red in tooth and claw.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson
discrepancy_note: Original is 'Tho' Nature, red in tooth and claw / With ravine, shriek'd against his creed' (Canto 56). The popular tag drops the surrounding theological context.

popular_quote: Do not go gentle into that good night. Rage, rage against the dying of the light.
author: Dylan Thomas
poem_title: Do Not Go Gentle into That Good Night
discrepancy_note: Identical to original (lines collapsed for popular sharing).

popular_quote: A thing of beauty is a joy forever.
author: John Keats

popular_quote: Beauty is truth, truth beauty — that is all ye know on earth, and all ye need to know.
author: John Keats
discrepancy_note: Identical to original; debated whether the urn or the poet speaks the line.
snippet_in_source: "Beauty is truth, truth beauty,—that is all

popular_quote: Full fathom five thy father lies; of his bones are coral made.
author: William Shakespeare

popular_quote: If you can keep your head when all about you are losing theirs and blaming it on you.
author: Rudyard Kipling
snippet_in_source: If you can keep your head when all about you

popular_quote: Oh, East is East, and West is West, and never the twain shall meet.
author: Rudyard Kipling
poem_title: The Ballad of East and West
discrepancy_note: Quote stops one couplet too early — Kipling continues 'But there is neither East nor West, Border, nor Breed, nor Birth, / When two strong men stand face to face...' inverting the usual reading.
snippet_in_source:
Oh, East is East, and West is West, and never the twain shall meet,
Till Earth and Sky stand presently at God's great Judgment Seat;
But there is neither East nor West, Border, nor Breed, nor Birth,
When two strong men stand face to face, though they come from the ends of the earth!

popular_quote: How do I love thee? Let me count the ways.
author: Elizabeth Barrett Browning
snippet_in_source: How do I love thee? Let me count the ways.

popular_quote: The mind is its own place, and in itself can make a Heaven of Hell, a Hell of Heaven.
author: John Milton
snippet_in_source: The mind is its own place, and in it self

popular_quote: Better to reign in Hell, than serve in Heaven.
author: John Milton
snippet_in_source: Better to reign in Hell, then serve in Heav'n.

popular_quote: Tomorrow to fresh fields and pastures new.
author: John Milton
discrepancy_note: Original is 'Tomorrow to fresh Woods, and Pastures new'; popular version substitutes 'fields' for 'Woods'.

popular_quote: What is this life if, full of care, we have no time to stand and stare?
author: W.H. Davies

popular_quote: I think that I shall never see a poem lovely as a tree.
author: Joyce Kilmer

popular_quote: When I am an old woman I shall wear purple.
author: Jenny Joseph

popular_quote: Dulce et decorum est pro patria mori.
author: Wilfred Owen
discrepancy_note: Owen quotes Horace's line ('It is sweet and fitting to die for one's country') only to call it 'the old Lie'. Popular usage often inverts Owen's intent and uses it sincerely.
snippet_in_source: The old Lie: Dulce et decorum est

popular_quote: In Flanders fields the poppies blow between the crosses, row on row.
author: John McCrae
snippet_in_source: In Flanders fields the poppies blow

popular_quote: Hell hath no fury like a woman scorned.
author: William Congreve
discrepancy_note: Original: 'Heaven has no rage like love to hatred turned, / Nor hell a fury like a woman scorned.' The popular tag radically condenses two lines into one.

popular_quote: The lady doth protest too much, methinks.
author: William Shakespeare
discrepancy_note: Often cited as 'Methinks the lady doth protest too much' — original word order is 'The lady doth protest too much, methinks.'
snippet_in_source:
QUEEN
2155
The lady doth protest too much, methinks.

popular_quote: Alas, poor Yorick! I knew him, Horatio.
author: William Shakespeare
discrepancy_note: Routinely misquoted as 'I knew him well'; original is 'I knew him, Horatio'.

popular_quote: To gild refined gold, to paint the lily.
author: William Shakespeare
discrepancy_note: Popular shorthand 'to gild the lily' fuses two distinct images from the original.
snippet_in_source:
1711
 To gild refinèd gold, to paint the lily,

popular_quote: If music be the food of love, play on.
author: William Shakespeare
snippet_in_source:
0001
 If music be the food of love, play on.

popular_quote: We few, we happy few, we band of brothers.
author: William Shakespeare
snippet_in_source:
2293
 We few, we happy few, we band of brothers;

popular_quote: Friends, Romans, countrymen, lend me your ears.
author: William Shakespeare
snippet_in_source:
1559
 Friends, Romans, countrymen, lend me your ears.

popular_quote: The quality of mercy is not strained.
author: William Shakespeare
snippet_in_source:
2099
190 The quality of mercy is not strained.

popular_quote: Romeo, Romeo, wherefore art thou Romeo?
author: William Shakespeare
discrepancy_note: Identical to original; commonly misread as 'where' (location) rather than 'why' — the line laments his name, not his absence.
snippet_in_source:
0859
 O Romeo, Romeo, wherefore art thou Romeo?

popular_quote: Double, double toil and trouble; fire burn and cauldron bubble.
author: William Shakespeare
snippet_in_source:
1488
10 Double, double toil and trouble;

popular_quote: To be, or not to be, that is the question.
author: William Shakespeare
snippet_in_source:
1762
 To be or not to be—that is the question:

popular_quote: Busy old fool, unruly Sun, why dost thou thus through windows and through curtains call on us?
author: John Donne
snippet_in_source: Busy old fool, unruly sun,

popular_quote: No man is an island, entire of itself.
author: John Donne
discrepancy_note: Often quoted as poetry but is from Donne's prose Meditations; the line is genuinely Donne's.

popular_quote: For whom the bell tolls; it tolls for thee.
author: John Donne
discrepancy_note: From Donne's prose Meditation, not a verse poem; popularized by Hemingway's novel title.

popular_quote: I celebrate myself, and sing myself, and what I assume you shall assume.
author: Walt Whitman
snippet_in_source: I celebrate myself, and sing myself,

popular_quote: Do I contradict myself? Very well then I contradict myself, (I am large, I contain multitudes.)
author: Walt Whitman
discrepancy_note: Identical to original; the parenthetical 'I contain multitudes' is part of the printed line.
snippet_in_source: Do I contradict myself?

popular_quote: O Captain! my Captain! our fearful trip is done.
author: Walt Whitman
snippet_in_source: O Captain! my Captain! our fearful trip is done,

popular_quote: I sing the body electric.
author: Walt Whitman
snippet_in_source: I sing the body electric,

popular_quote: Tell me, what is it you plan to do with your one wild and precious life?
author: Mary Oliver
discrepancy_note: Identical to original; commonly excerpted as standalone motivational sound-bite, but in context the question follows praise of doing nothing ('strolling through the fields all day').

popular_quote: You only have to let the soft animal of your body love what it loves.
author: Mary Oliver

popular_quote: You may write me down in history with your bitter, twisted lies, you may trod me in the very dirt but still, like dust, I'll rise.
author: Maya Angelou
snippet_in_source: You may write me down in history

popular_quote: I'm a woman, phenomenally. Phenomenal woman, that's me.
author: Maya Angelou

popular_quote: Dying is an art, like everything else. I do it exceptionally well.
author: Sylvia Plath

popular_quote: I eat men like air.
author: Sylvia Plath
snippet_in_source: And I eat men like air.

popular_quote: Drink to me only with thine eyes, and I will pledge with mine.
author: Ben Jonson

popular_quote: Music I heard with you was more than music, and bread I broke with you was more than bread.
author: Conrad Aiken

popular_quote: And the sunlight clasps the earth, and the moonbeams kiss the sea: what are all these kissings worth, if thou kiss not me?
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

popular_quote: Hear the sledges with the bells — silver bells! What a world of merriment their melody foretells!
author: Edgar Allan Poe

popular_quote: Quoth the Raven "Nevermore."
author: Edgar Allan Poe
snippet_in_source: Quoth the Raven “Nevermore.”

popular_quote: Once upon a midnight dreary, while I pondered, weak and weary.
author: Edgar Allan Poe
snippet_in_source: Once upon a midnight dreary, while I pondered, weak and weary,

popular_quote: The moving finger writes; and, having writ, moves on.
author: Omar Khayyam (trans. Edward FitzGerald)
discrepancy_note: FitzGerald's translation has become more famous than the Persian original; Khayyam's quatrains do not have a fixed canonical form, and FitzGerald's loose paraphrase has near-canonical status in English.
snippet_in_source:
The Moving Finger writes; and, having writ,
 Moves on: nor all thy Piety nor Wit
 Shall lure it back to cancel half a Line,
 Nor all thy Tears wash out a Word of it.

popular_quote: A jug of wine, a loaf of bread — and thou.
author: Omar Khayyam (trans. Edward FitzGerald)
discrepancy_note: FitzGerald's loose English version; the Persian original is structurally different and is largely a creative reworking.
snippet_in_source:
A Book of Verses underneath the Bough,
 A Jug of Wine, a Loaf of Bread—and Thou
 Beside me singing in the Wilderness—
 Oh, Wilderness were Paradise enow!

popular_quote: I love you without knowing how, or when, or from where. I love you simply, without problems or pride.
author: Pablo Neruda
discrepancy_note: English version (Stephen Tapscott trans.) of 'No te amo como si fueras rosa de sal'; widely shared in English without translator credit.

popular_quote: I want to do with you what spring does with the cherry trees.
author: Pablo Neruda
poem_title: Twenty Love Poems and a Song of Despair (Poem XIV)
discrepancy_note: Translation of 'Quiero hacer contigo lo que la primavera hace con los cerezos'; English form is W.S. Merwin's translation, often shared without attribution.

popular_quote: Tonight I can write the saddest lines.
author: Pablo Neruda
poem_title: Twenty Love Poems and a Song of Despair (Poem XX)
discrepancy_note: Translation of 'Puedo escribir los versos más tristes esta noche'; English version commonly cited.
snippet_in_source: Tonight I Can Write The Saddest Lines

popular_quote: You must change your life.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
discrepancy_note: English of 'Du musst dein Leben ändern' (Stephen Mitchell trans.); identical to original closing line.

popular_quote: Perhaps all the dragons in our lives are princesses who are only waiting to see us act, just once, with beauty and courage.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
poem_title: Letters to a Young Poet (Letter Eight)
discrepancy_note: From Rilke's prose 'Briefe an einen jungen Dichter', not a poem; popularly shared as poetry.

popular_quote: Edel sei der Mensch, hilfreich und gut!
author: Johann Wolfgang von Goethe
discrepancy_note: Identical to original — 'Let man be noble, helpful and good'.

popular_quote: Wer reitet so spät durch Nacht und Wind? Es ist der Vater mit seinem Kind.
author: Johann Wolfgang von Goethe

popular_quote: Über allen Gipfeln ist Ruh.
author: Johann Wolfgang von Goethe
discrepancy_note: Identical to original — 'Over all the peaks is peace'. Often described as the most famous lyric in German.

popular_quote: Wo aber Gefahr ist, wächst das Rettende auch.
author: Friedrich Hölderlin
discrepancy_note: Identical to original — 'But where danger is, grows the saving power also'.

popular_quote: Les sanglots longs des violons de l'automne blessent mon cœur d'une langueur monotone.
author: Paul Verlaine
discrepancy_note: Identical to original; the BBC famously used these lines as a coded D-Day signal in 1944.

popular_quote: Je est un autre.
author: Arthur Rimbaud
poem_title: Lettre du voyant (Letter to Paul Demeny)
discrepancy_note: From Rimbaud's prose letter, not a poem proper; the line ('I is another') has become his most-quoted utterance.

popular_quote: Demain, dès l'aube, à l'heure où blanchit la campagne, je partirai.
author: Victor Hugo
discrepancy_note: Identical to original; written on the anniversary of his daughter Léopoldine's death.

popular_quote: Hypocrite lecteur, — mon semblable, — mon frère!
author: Charles Baudelaire
discrepancy_note: Identical to original — 'Hypocrite reader — my likeness, my brother!' Quoted in T.S. Eliot's The Waste Land.

popular_quote: Odi et amo. Quare id faciam, fortasse requiris. Nescio, sed fieri sentio et excrucior.
author: Catullus
discrepancy_note: Identical to original — 'I hate and I love'. The complete two-line poem; commonly quoted only as the opening 'Odi et amo'.
snippet_in_source: Odi et amo. quare id faciam, fortasse requiris.

popular_quote: Omnia vincit Amor; et nos cedamus Amori.
author: Virgil
discrepancy_note: Identical to original — 'Love conquers all; let us too yield to Love'.

popular_quote: Carpe diem, quam minimum credula postero.
author: Horace
discrepancy_note: Often shortened to 'Carpe diem' = 'seize the day'; the fuller phrase is 'pluck the day, trusting tomorrow as little as possible' — closer to a memento mori than the modern hustle slogan.
snippet_in_source: aetas: carpe diem, quam minimum credula postero.

popular_quote: Exegi monumentum aere perennius.
author: Horace
discrepancy_note: Identical to original — 'I have raised a monument more lasting than bronze'.
snippet_in_source: Exegi monumentum aere perennius

popular_quote: Sing, O goddess, the anger of Achilles son of Peleus.
author: Homer
discrepancy_note: English of 'μῆνιν ἄειδε θεὰ Πηληϊάδεω Ἀχιλῆος'; the standard Lattimore/Fagles opening. Many translations differ in word choice (rage, wrath, anger).

popular_quote: Sing to me of the man, Muse, the man of twists and turns.
author: Homer
discrepancy_note: Robert Fagles translation; the Greek 'πολύτροπον' literally 'of many turns' has been rendered very differently across translators — itself a study in canonical variance.

popular_quote: Little snail, slowly, slowly, climb Mount Fuji.
author: Kobayashi Issa
discrepancy_note: English of 蝸牛そろそろ登れ富士の山; popular Robert Hass version — Issa wrote no fixed English text.

popular_quote: The country is broken; mountains and rivers remain. Spring in the city — grass and trees grow deep.
author: Du Fu
discrepancy_note: Translation of 國破山河在，城春草木深; the Chinese original packs this into 10 characters and is among the most-quoted couplets in Chinese.
snippet_in_source: The country is broken, though hills and rivers remain,

popular_quote: Out, out, brief candle! Life's but a walking shadow.
author: William Shakespeare
snippet_in_source:
2285
 The way to dusty death. Out, out, brief candle!
p.
 179

popular_quote: Tomorrow, and tomorrow, and tomorrow, creeps in this petty pace from day to day.
author: William Shakespeare
snippet_in_source:
2281
 Tomorrow and tomorrow and tomorrow

popular_quote: All the world's a stage, and all the men and women merely players.
author: William Shakespeare
snippet_in_source:
JAQUES
1104
 All the world’s a stage,

popular_quote: Anyone lived in a pretty how town.
author: E.E. Cummings
poem_title: anyone lived in a pretty how town
snippet_in_source: anyone lived in a pretty how town

popular_quote: i carry your heart with me (i carry it in my heart).
author: E.E. Cummings
poem_title: [i carry your heart with me(i carry it in]
discrepancy_note: Identical to original; original includes Cummings's signature spacing/parentheses.

popular_quote: On either side the river lie long fields of barley and of rye.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson
snippet_in_source: On either side the river lie

popular_quote: I will arise and go now, and go to Innisfree.
author: W.B. Yeats
snippet_in_source: I will arise and go now, and go to Innisfree,

popular_quote: I have eaten the plums that were in the icebox.
author: William Carlos Williams
discrepancy_note: Identical to original; the source of the long-running 'plums meme'.
snippet_in_source: I have eaten

popular_quote: Good fences make good neighbours.
author: Robert Frost
discrepancy_note: Often cited as Frost's own moral; in the poem the line is the neighbour's saying that the speaker doubts.
snippet_in_source: He only says, ‘Good fences make good neighbors.’

popular_quote: First they came for the socialists, and I did not speak out — because I was not a socialist.
author: Martin Niemöller
discrepancy_note: No single canonical version — Niemöller spoke this in many forms; the popular English text varies in which groups appear and in what order.
snippet_in_source: First they came for the socialists, and I did not speak out—because I was not a socialist.

popular_quote: Footprints in the sands of time.
author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow

popular_quote: Listen, my children, and you shall hear of the midnight ride of Paul Revere.
author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
